title: Changing Behavior by Extinguishing or Punishing It

Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 15   (I. E. Handouts 21–22)
Changing Behavior by Extinguishing or Punishing It
Due Date:             Name:                                    Week Starting:
Fill out this sheet whenever you try to increase your own or someone else’s behavior with reinforce-
ment. Look for opportunities (since they occur all the time) to reinforce behavior. Write on the back
of this sheet if you need more room.
1.	 In advance, identify the behavior you want to decrease, and decide whether you will
extinguish it by eliminating a reinforcer or stop it with punishment. (Skip the one you are
not using.)
If you are using punishment, identify the consequence. Also decide the new alternative
behavior to reinforce, and the reinforcer to use to increase it to replace the behavior you are
decreasing.
a.	 For yourself:
Behavior to decrease:
Reinforcer to remove:
Punishing consequence to add:
New behavior and reinforcer:
b.	 For someone else:
Behavior to decrease:
Reinforcer to remove:
Punishing consequence to add:
New behavior and reinforcer:
2.	 Describe the situation(s) where you used extinction or punishment. (Circle which you
use.)
a.	 For yourself:
b.	 For someone else:
3.	 What was the outcome? What did you observe?
a.	 For yourself:


b.	 For someone else:


4.	 How did you feel afterward?


5.	 Would you do something differently next time? If so, what?

197
Emotion
Regulation Skills
Introduction to Handouts and Worksheets
The goal of emotion regulation is to reduce emotional suffering. The goal is not
to get rid of emotions; emotions have important functions in our lives. Emotion
regulation skills help you to change emotions that you (not other people) want to
change, or to reduce the intensity of your emotions. Emotion regulation skills can
also reduce your vulnerability to becoming extremely or painfully emotional and
increase your emotional resilience. Emotion regulation requires use of mindfulness
skills, particularly nonjudgmental observation and description of your own current
emotions. You have to know what an emotion is and what it does for you before you
can effectively regulate it.
There are four sets of handouts and worksheets for emotion regulation skills:
Understanding and Naming Emotions; Changing Emotional Responses; Reducing
Vulnerability to Emotion Mind; and Managing Really Difficult Emotions. There is
also one introductory handout–worksheet pair:
•• Emotion Regulation Handout 1: Goals of Emotion Regulation. This handout
briefly outlines the goals of the skills taught in this module. It can be used with Emo-
tion Regulation Worksheet 1: Pros and Cons of Changing Emotions.
Understanding and Naming Emotions
•• Emotion Regulation Handout 2: Overview: Understanding and Naming
Emotions. It is difficult to manage your emotions when you do not understand how
emotions work. Knowledge is power. This handout overviews the skills covered in
this section.
•• Emotion Regulation Handout 3: What Emotions Do for You. There are

ourspace_shared_life_filter.title: Changing Behavior by Extinguishing or Punishing It
ourspace_shared_life_filter.shared_facing_summary: Use this to protect both people: needs are real, boundaries are kind, and a pause is not abandonment when repair is named.
tiny_steps: Name the ask in one sentence.
Agree on a return time or next check-in.

flare_day_versions.minimum: One breath, one label, one safe message, or one checkbox.
flare_day_versions.medium: Two to five minutes with everything reachable from bed/chair.
flare_day_versions.full: Use the original worksheet/handout when both bodies and brains have capacity.

accessibility_modifications: Use text/chat if speech is hard.
Include caregiver capacity as part of the plan, not an afterthought.
relationship_and_caregiver_notes: William's needs and Jasper's capacity both count.
Ask before helping; ask before escalating; name the smallest next step.
Pause means pause, not abandonment. I am coming back. I care about us. I need a smaller next step.
What is needed, what capacity exists, what can be smaller, and what can wait?
identity_and_trauma_filter: Use William's masculine language and Jasper's they/them language.
Use consent-based, non-shaming, body-neutral language unless a person has explicitly chosen a term.
Do not turn symptoms, dysphoria, trauma responses, clutter, fatigue, or emotional intensity into moral failure.
avoid_or_adjust: Do not use skills to win, corner, threaten leaving, or make either person prove pain or disability.
